title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Idan Raman: The Identity Crisis of Browser Agents
transcript: Today I'll be speaking about >> [music] >> what we call the identity crisis for browser agents or how can we take human identities and let AI agents use them in a way which is very secure and reliable. So, just a bit about Anchor and about me before we get going. So, I'm Idan Goldman. I'm one of the co-founders and CEO of Anchor. Um Anchor is focused on secure infrastructure for all computer using agents. So, any agent that wants to be able to act on a computer just like a human should be using Anchor to get very reliable, very secure access to a computer. We've been partnering with tech leaders like Cloudflare, 1Password, fingerprint.com, and Coin-Coinbase. Um and you can use this QR code to get to Anchor's website. Okay. So, starting with the high-level question is, how can we let AI agents use human accounts in a very secure and reliable way? And the story here is both the story about the technology itself, but also about something that we call in Anchor complexity creep within any technology that you build into your organization. Um so, at any technology there is sort of the perceived complexity and what you think is going to be the challenges and problems when you just get going, which is the tip of the iceberg, so to speak. But then there is quite a long uh and deep rabbit hole of what you might get into after you start implementing and onboarding this technology. And I think in identity and in computer using agents in particular, um this aspect is really pronounced and we see that even for us when we started Anchor, we believed identity to be quite easy, quite simple. And the more we dug in, we just saw how much complexity there is in taking 20 years of human identity methods and credentials, and sort of retrofitting it retrofitting it to be available for AI agents. So, before we dive into the actual identity technology, I'm going to start with a story about a pretty big mistake we did strategic strategically in the tech stack within Ankr when we just got started. So, we decided when we just started to implement Ankr to build our own metering and billing system to monitor how much usage we see from end customers, um what their bill should be, and then bill it to them through Stripe. Um and very early on, it seemed like a very simple problem. We're just going to uh monitor the usage every month uh by the month, we're going to see how much usage there were, uh and send out invoices, and it's going to be fun and good and great. But, the more we dug into this problem, we got to these edge cases where you want to either give a discount, do rollover of credits, do annual deals, um make all these changes and adaptations to the pricing model and the billing model. And the more we did it, we effectively just built a full billing system into Ankr all by ourselves. And it's the worst piece of technology that we have to maintain right now. And we're really looking forward, and this isn't paid advertisement, but we're switching over from this billing system to Metronome, um which is a great product uh that Stripe also owns. So, this was sort of our own um complexity creep and complexity creep mistake where we thought this is going to be very simple. This is just going to be one and done. We're going to have this billing system figured out. But the more that we use it, we just saw this is not something that we should own in house. And what we see is that the exact same pattern comes back and is really, really accurate for human authentication for AI agents. And I'm going to try to show you on this presentation just how far this rabbit hole goes and how much complexity there is in human authentication for these agents. So, the tip of the iceberg, the most naive approach, what we see most customers onboarding themselves building their on their own, um, is the very naive approach of saying, um, actually giving the agent a prompt with the credentials, telling it, "Please log me in with this username and this password to this website." Um, which is very vibe Cody. It's very nice. It actually works for some of the use cases. Um, obviously, once you go into high scale, secure, enterprise workloads, when you're handling credentials for your end users, you never want to do that. This is very insecure and you're exposing credentials to LLMs uh that are outside of your control. So, the the first sort of, uh, diving into the water is what we see in the market right now, it's sort of the standard in the market, is instead of giving the credentials plain text to the agent, there is an obfuscation be done of the credentials. So, this is what we show here. Um, it's so kind of a naive better approach, but it still falls short. So, instead of saying to the agent, "These are the username and the password," I just give them, uh, a mock data, like, uh, "Curly braces username, curly braces password, please use that." And then I can switch that in runtime with the actual credentials. This is a bit better because the agent can't see the credentials when it fills them in, but actually after the agent fills them in and parses the webpage, it again has the has access to the credentials. So, even this system of storing the credentials in a vault, pulling them in, and kind of obfuscating them to the agent doesn't really work in practice and it still exposes you to a lot of security issues and leaking the credentials effectively. So, that's one one pillar of the problem. Going over to another pillar of the problem, um to even get going with identity and authenticate authentication to get logged in, you always need to get through the barrier of are you a bot? Are you uh a able to access the website? Um almost every website today has some sort of a web application firewall or a CAPTCHA. Um so, we actually had to build a full stack just to solve this problem of getting logged in depends on bot detection and bot detection mechanisms. We partnered with companies like Cloudflare and fingerprint.com that are big detection mechanisms to have Anchor agents be verified agents within the browser. And that means that every time you visit a website that is protected by Cloudflare, we're able to be a verified good bot that logs in instead of being blocked by these systems. And obviously, even for Google reCAPTCHA and any other CAPTCHA method, we're able to very reliably and effectively uh go through them and still get you to the actual website. After you get through the CAPTCHA, another very important uh method in the sort of identity ecosystem is your network fingerprint and your IP address. So, going into any website, if you're using just a VM, like a virtual machine and AWS IP address, most likely you're going to get blocked from accessing that website. Websites will just blocklist every IP address that comes out of AWS. So, most vendors in the market to get you authenticated, to get you logged in, use third-party proxy solutions. Third-party proxies are this huge enormous industry of uh millions of devices all over the world that are being used to give you high-quality IP addresses, but unfortunately, they're not an enterprise-ready solution. Um when you use a third-party uh proxy um like the I won't say specific vendor names, but the largest proxy networks in the world actually rely on uh TV uh smart TVs, like a smart Samsung TV, uh personal mobile devices, phone devices, to get you uh this network access. And this network access effectively means that all of your network traffic goes through unknown third-party devices. So, it's both very unsecure, it's unreliable, and you're getting every single time a different IP address. Now, for any organization that uses SSO and Okta, if you get a a different IP address every time you try to get logged in, you're immediately going to be flagged as a bot and as a adversary instead of being um detected as a good actor and a good behaving uh bot. What we actually had to do to account and to enable reliable identity access is to build our own VPN. So, we actually built from the ground up an enterprise VPN in partnership with Telco companies. So, we bought data center locations with companies like AT&T to be able to give very high quality IP addresses that actually belong to our end customers. And that way, they get the entire stack, the bot detection and IP address to make sure that they're able to access any website in a reliable way. Now, going back to the actual main piece of the puzzle, which is the actual authentication, we started with a username and password, which is great for very naive authentication methods. But nowadays, 2026, almost any authentication is also dependent on MFA, on SSO, on passkeys, on magic links. All of these technologies are so much more complex than the naive uh use the username and a password to get logged in. So, what we actually had to do in Anchor, we built the equivalent of MFA for an agent. So, our agents can have a secure way to integrate to any authentication portal that requires you to have an MFA. We're also able to use SSO, passkeys, and magic links. And the list goes on and on. So, for any new authentication factor that you'll see out in the wild, you'll either have to build it on your own if you're building your own identity solution, or if you're relying on a vendor, you probably should ask them and make sure that they support every single um authentication factor that you might might encounter. Now, I think one one major sort of realization and change in the way that we um perceive identity and authentication was when we stopped looking at it through the lens of it's a one transaction thing, so you just get logged in. This This is just one piece of the cycle. And authentication is actually a full life cycle rather than just one transaction. Every authentication depends on onboarding the credentials as the first step, then using them um for however many agent runs you're going to do, and then also handling failures and fallbacks, and what happens if the credential changes or the scope of the access is changed. So, this whole life cycle of how do I not just give credentials and hopefully wishfully sort of happy path believe that they're just going to work forever. Uh how do I move from that position and start to look at the identity and authentication as the full life cycle of onboarding, um validating, using, and then keeping that credentials alive and uh fixing them whenever something has changed in the scope or the credentials themselves. And this is just a taste of what we've started to see when we uh got underwater and started to look into this iceberg of identity and authentication technology. We still see so much more, and I feel like every time we dive deeper, we just see more and more capabilities that to have effective agents that can use human credentials in a reliable way, you just need to go through these uh patterns to make sure that your uh identity stack is actually viable. And I think going back to the beginning of this presentation, when we asked ourselves, why is it so hard to onboard AI agents to the enterprise? And why it takes so long, and why is it so not trivial to have millions of agents onboarded for everyone all at once. Um we need to remember that any piece of the agent puzzle has the complexity very similar to what we've shown in this presentation. So, the diffusion and actually inserting agents into any piece of what we use and what we build is a very complex, very long process. And even if we look into pieces of technology like Open Claw, Open Claw feels like this uh all doing all able, it can connect to anything, it could do a- anything. But obviously, to deploy Open Claw like capabilities for the enterprise, you need to figure out authentication. You need to figure out how you're going to give out the credentials, how you're going to connect human identities to these Open Claw instances. And through that, you also need to account for uh guardrails and authorization and monitoring. Um I think only by looking into each and every piece of your infrastructure stack and building the foundation, you're going to be able to have a very reliable, very robust, effective onboarding of agents to to organizations. Um so, I'm I'm a space geek. And I really like SpaceX like all space geeks. And this is a 90-minutes video. We're not going to see it today, but it's a 90-minute video that I really recommend um about how SpaceX built their stage zero for their new uh launch vehicle. And what they show is that for 8 months, they actually had to pile sand on top of the swamps in Florida uh and then pour tons of concrete, like millions of tons of concrete to make the foundations on which they were able to build uh sort of the launch uh mount for this missile. And I think AI infrastructure and identity infrastructure is very similar in the sense that to have effective, reliable, reusable identities, you need um to build the foundations in a very deep way, in a very reliable way, starting from first principles and looking into every piece of the stack in a very scrutinizing manner. So, how do I make sure my network layer works well, my bots bypass works well well or bot verification, and the actual authentication works well because they all need to work in tandem for authentication to actually work for these agents. Yeah, so but what I hopefully convinced you today or showed you today is why you should probably not own your authentication infrastructure for agents and why you should probably find vendors and solutions either open source or products that you can use to have effective authentication for your AI agents.
